clean_story: Listen, brother of the fire—this is cold stone that does not come from the earth. It is born when men take the black blood of dead beasts (the stinky ooze deep below ground) and cook it in great fires until it weeps like sap. The liquid hardens into clear or colored skins—smooth as river-worn pebble, but lighter than bone, stronger than dried hide. It does not rot like plant-fiber, nor shatter like flint; even water fears it, sliding off like rain on duck feathers. If you bite it, your teeth will slip—no taste, no give, like gnawing sun-bleached antler. When fire licks it, it melts into foul-smoking tears, not ash. The outsiders shape it into endless things: bowls that never crack, ropes that never fray, even fake animal skins that do not breathe. Beware—it lives forever, yet feeds nothing. The land spits it back up like a bad berry.
